title: Conformally Invariant Fields out of Brownian Loop Soups
speaker: Wei Qian
abstract: For each central charge c∈(0,1], we construct a conformally invariant field which is a measurable function of the local time field L of the Brownian loop soup with intensity c and i.i.d. signs given to each cluster. This field is canonically associated to L, in a sense which is similar to the isomorphism theory that associates the Gaussian free field to the loop soup with critical intensity. Isomorphisms between Brownian motions and random fields were previously developed by Symanzik, Brydges-Fröhlich-Spencer, Dynkin and Le Jan in several different settings.

proof_stages.label: Brownian loop soup and cluster background
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the planar Brownian loop measure and Poisson loop soup, records conformal invariance, and reviews the cluster phase transition and CLE description of outer boundaries.

proof_stages.label: Critical local-time isomorphism and continuum obstacles
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the Le Jan/Symanzik occupation-time isomorphism with the GFF, explains the need for renormalization in two dimensions, and uses the cable-graph case to show why recovering the field from signed clusters is nontrivial in the continuum and subcritical regimes.

proof_stages.label: Signed multiplicative-chaos construction
proof_stages.summary: Loop-soup multiplicative chaos is introduced, iid signs are assigned to clusters, the positive signed chaos measure is centered and normalized, and the main convergence theorem for the fields H_{theta,gamma} is stated.

proof_stages.label: Critical-intensity calibration by the GFF
proof_stages.summary: At theta=1/2 the construction is checked against the known GFF isomorphism: the total chaos is a cosh of the GFF, the signed restrictions are exponentials of ±gamma times the GFF, and the gamma-to-zero limit recovers the Gaussian free field.

proof_stages.label: Basic properties of the subcritical field
proof_stages.summary: For all subcritical theta the limiting field is shown to have a log-power covariance singularity, conformal invariance, and sign symmetry, with the Q&A emphasizing that only the current asymptotic covariance is known.

proof_stages.label: Second-moment reduction to cluster connectivity
proof_stages.summary: The proof of convergence is organized through second moments of H_{theta,gamma}(f), using correlation identities for multiplicative chaos that add rooted loop clouds at the sampled points and reduce the limit to cluster connection probabilities.

proof_stages.label: Cluster crossing exponent and geometric consequences
proof_stages.summary: The needed cluster connectivity input is supplied by a subcritical crossing exponent of order (log 1/epsilon)^{-1+theta}, leading also to a cluster conditioned to pass through a point and to Minkowski-gauge estimates for clusters.

proof_stages.label: Fractional-power isomorphism heuristics and conjectures
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes a discrete-loop-soup approximation using signed occupation time to the power 1−theta, relates it to conjectural series expansions for the signed chaos, and gives a one-dimensional Bessel-process calculation as evidence for the fractional-power isomorphism.

examples.name: Bounded-domain loop-soup simulation
examples.note: The displayed bounded-domain configuration makes concrete the facts that only finitely many large loops but infinitely many small dense loops occur, and later shows the subcritical cluster picture and outer boundaries.
examples.engagement_reason: the bounded-domain simulation is used illustratively while the speaker only states qualitative features like finitely many large loops and infinitely many small loops

examples.name: Full-plane loop soup contrast
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts bounded-domain soups with the full-plane case, where large loops force essentially one cluster, clarifying why the theory is formulated in a proper domain.
examples.engagement_reason: the full-plane case is contrasted by stating that big loops create one infinite cluster for any intensity, without deriving or analyzing it further

passes.reason: The full-plane loop soup is the unbounded limiting domain and is invoked to show the bounded-domain phase-transition picture does not extend there.

examples.name: Cable-graph approximation
examples.note: The cable graph with continuous edges illustrates a setting where local time is a finite function and the square-root plus iid-sign recipe exactly recovers the GFF, motivating the harder continuum question.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the loop-soup/GFF construction on the cable graph, specifying Brownian motion on edges, vertex transitions, finite occupation times, square roots, cluster signs, and recovery of the GFF

passes.reason: The cable graph is a standard interpolation/approximation used constructively to make local times finite and recover the GFF.

examples.name: Subcritical percolation comparison
examples.note: The comparison with subcritical percolation highlights that loop-soup cluster connection probabilities decay only by powers of logarithms rather than at the much faster rates expected in ordinary subcritical models.
examples.engagement_reason: subcritical percolation is only invoked as a comparison for faster decay, with no calculation or construction performed on it

examples.name: One-dimensional loop soup and squared Bessel processes
examples.note: The one-dimensional BESQ description is used to reveal why the signed fractional power, rather than the square root, is the natural subcritical analogue through a dual-dimension weighting identity.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the one-dimensional loop soup via squared Bessel processes, using additivity, dimensions, zero-hitting behavior, fractional powers, signs, and a dual-dimension weighting identity

passes.reason: The one-dimensional loop-soup/BESQ model is a minimal base case where local times are actual functions, used for intuition about the fractional power.
